rejectionReason: We discovered one or more bugs in your app. Your app crashed during our review.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app that had a race condition in the initialization sequence. The app worked fine locally but crashed on first launch when network conditions were slower, which matched Apple's review environment.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We discovered one or more bugs in your app when reviewed on iPhone 15 Pro running iOS 17.2. Specifically, the app crashed on launch. Please review the crash logs attached in the resolution center.
fix: Analyzed the crash log from the resolution center. Found a force-unwrap on an optional that was nil when a network request hadn't completed before the UI tried to access the data. Added proper nil-checking and async/await handling for the initialization sequence. Tested on a clean install with network throttling enabled.
behavioralSignals: works locally but crashes in review

rejectionReason: Your app includes placeholder content. Apps should be complete and fully functional when submitted for review.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app where some secondary screens still contained 'Lorem ipsum' placeholder text, sample images, and TODO comments visible in the UI. The developer forgot to replace placeholder content before submission.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: Your app includes placeholder content that makes it appear as though the app is not yet complete. Specifically, we found Lorem ipsum text on the About page and placeholder images in the settings screen. Please complete all content before resubmitting.
fix: Replaced all Lorem ipsum text with real content. Replaced placeholder images with production assets. Did a full audit of every screen to ensure no remaining placeholder content, TODO labels, or sample data was visible.

rejectionReason: Your app references TestFlight or beta testing in the production build.
whatDeveloperDid: Left a 'Thank you for beta testing!' banner and a 'Report Beta Bug' button visible in the production build. The app also had a settings toggle labeled 'TestFlight Debug Mode' that was visible to all users.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: Your app contains references to TestFlight and beta testing, which is not appropriate for the production version of your app. Specifically, we found a 'Report Beta Bug' button and references to TestFlight in the settings. Please remove all beta-related content.
fix: Wrapped all beta/TestFlight-related UI elements in compile-time flags (#if DEBUG) and runtime checks for TestFlight environment. Removed the beta testing banner and debug toggle from the production build configuration.

rejectionReason: Your app does not work on IPv6-only networks as required.
whatDeveloperDid: Hardcoded IPv4 addresses for API server connections instead of using domain names. Apple's review network uses a NAT64/DNS64 environment (IPv6-only), causing all direct IPv4 connections to fail.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We discovered one or more bugs in your app when reviewed on iPad running iOS 17.1 on an IPv6 network. Specifically, the app was unable to load content. Apps must support IPv6-only networking as many carrier networks are IPv6-only.
fix: Replaced all hardcoded IPv4 addresses with domain name-based connections. Used NSURLSession/URLSession which handles IPv6 natively. Tested using Apple's NAT64 test network setup (macOS Internet Sharing with 'Create NAT64 Network' enabled) to verify connectivity.
behavioralSignals: works on WiFi but fails in review

rejectionReason: Your app failed to connect to its server when tested on Apple's IPv6 review network.
whatDeveloperDid: The app used a third-party networking library that had a bug with NAT64 address synthesis. It worked on the developer's local IPv6 test setup but failed on Apple's specific NAT64/DNS64 configuration because the library was manually resolving DNS instead of letting the system handle it.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: Your app was unable to connect to its server during review. The app displayed a 'Connection Error' message when attempting to load the main feed. We tested on an IPv6 network and were unable to use the app's core functionality.
fix: Updated the third-party networking library to the latest version which fixed the NAT64 compatibility issue. Switched from manual DNS resolution to system-provided APIs (getaddrinfo) that properly handle IPv6 address synthesis. Verified using Apple's recommended NAT64 testing setup.
behavioralSignals: works locally but fails in review

rejectionReason: Your app declares audio background mode but does not play audio content.
whatDeveloperDid: Added the UIBackgroundModes 'audio' capability to keep the app alive in the background for periodic data syncing, even though the app had no audio playback functionality.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.5.4 - Performance - Software Requirements: Your app declares the audio background mode in UIBackgroundModes but does not include audio playback functionality. The audio background mode is intended only for apps that provide audible content to the user. Using background modes for purposes other than their intended function is not permitted.
fix: Removed the 'audio' UIBackgroundMode. Implemented proper background data syncing using BGTaskScheduler (BGAppRefreshTask and BGProcessingTask) which is the correct API for periodic background work without requiring audio playback.

rejectionReason: We were unable to sign in to your app with the demo account provided because it required two-factor authentication.
whatDeveloperDid: Provided demo account credentials in the App Review notes, but the account had mandatory two-factor authentication enabled which sent an SMS code to the developer's phone. The Apple reviewer had no way to receive the 2FA code.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We were unable to complete the review of your app because we could not sign in using the demo credentials provided. The account requires two-factor authentication via SMS, and we were unable to receive the verification code. Please provide demo credentials that allow full access without requiring additional verification steps.
fix: Created a dedicated demo/review account with 2FA disabled. Added the demo credentials (username, password) in the App Review Information section of App Store Connect. Included a note explaining which features to test and any special instructions for the reviewer.
behavioralSignals: unable to sign in during review

rejectionReason: We were unable to sign in using the magic link authentication because the link expired before we could use it.
whatDeveloperDid: Implemented passwordless authentication using magic links sent via email. When the Apple reviewer opened the email on iPad, the Mail app's link preview/prefetch feature automatically triggered the magic link URL, consuming it before the reviewer could manually tap it to authenticate.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We were unable to complete the login process. We received the magic link email, but when we tapped the link it showed 'Link expired or already used.' We attempted this multiple times with the same result.
fix: Changed the magic link flow to a two-step process: the link now opens a confirmation page in the browser where the user must tap 'Confirm Sign In' before the token is consumed. This prevents email client prefetching from invalidating the link. Also added a fallback OTP code option as an alternative to magic links.

rejectionReason: Passkey creation failed during our review. The app displayed an error when attempting to create a passkey.
whatDeveloperDid: Implemented passkey (WebAuthn) authentication but the Apple App Site Association (AASA) file wasn't being fetched quickly enough by Apple's CDN during the review. The passkey creation requires the AASA file to be available at /.well-known/apple-app-site-association to verify the domain-app association.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We were unable to create a passkey during review. When attempting to register, the app displayed 'Unable to create passkey. Please try again later.' We attempted multiple times without success.
fix: Ensured the AASA file was properly hosted and cached before submission. Added the associated domains entitlement with the webcredentials service. Pre-warmed the AASA cache by making requests to the domain before submission. Also added a fallback email/password registration option so reviewers could complete onboarding even if passkey creation failed.

rejectionReason: Your app includes the ability to download and execute code after installation, which can be used to introduce features not reviewed by Apple.
whatDeveloperDid: Used Microsoft CodePush (or a similar hot-code-push solution) to push JavaScript bundle updates to a React Native app, bypassing the App Store review process. The updates were capable of changing app functionality beyond minor bug fixes.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3.1 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: Your app contains hidden features. Specifically, your app includes functionality that allows for the remote downloading and execution of code that can change the app's behavior or functionality after review. Apps must not provide mechanisms to alter their primary functionality post-review.
fix: Configured CodePush to only push minor bug fixes and UI text changes, not functional changes. Added documentation in the review notes explaining that CodePush is used only for critical bug fixes and does not alter app functionality. Some developers choose to remove CodePush entirely and use standard App Store updates instead.

rejectionReason: Your app is missing the required app icon.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app without including the 1024x1024 App Store icon in the asset catalog, or the icon was corrupted/transparent. The app appeared with a blank or default icon in App Store Connect.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: Your app is missing the required 1024x1024 App Store icon. All apps must include a valid app icon that will be displayed on the App Store. Please add the icon to your app's asset catalog.
fix: Added the 1024x1024 App Store icon to the Assets.xcassets/AppIcon.appiconset. Verified all required icon sizes were present and none were transparent or contained alpha channels (which App Store Connect rejects).

rejectionReason: Your macOS app's release notes reference iOS-specific features and terminology.
whatDeveloperDid: Jesse Squires' Taxatio app was a universal app supporting both iOS and macOS. The release notes for a macOS update mentioned iOS-specific features like 'improved iPhone widget' and 'haptic feedback improvements' that don't apply to macOS.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: Your Mac app's release notes reference iOS-specific features ('iPhone widget', 'haptic feedback'). Release notes for macOS apps should be relevant to the macOS version of your app.
fix: Created platform-specific release notes. For macOS submissions, wrote release notes that only reference macOS-relevant features and improvements. Used App Store Connect's platform-specific metadata fields to provide different release notes for each platform.
behavioralSignals: iOS terms in macOS release notes
platform-specific features mentioned for wrong platform

rejectionReason: Your app's screenshots do not accurately reflect the current version of your app.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted an app update with significant UI changes but forgot to update the App Store screenshots. The screenshots showed the old design with a tab bar at the bottom, while the new version used a sidebar navigation pattern.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3.7 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: Your app's screenshots do not reflect the current version of your app. The screenshots show a different navigation pattern and UI layout than what is present in the current build. Please update your screenshots to accurately represent the app.
fix: Took new screenshots on all required device sizes reflecting the current UI. Used Xcode's screenshot automation or a tool like fastlane snapshot to generate consistent screenshots across all required device sizes and localizations.

rejectionReason: Your app preview poster frame contains the word 'Free' which references pricing.
whatDeveloperDid: Created an app preview video whose poster frame (the still image shown before the video plays) contained text overlay saying 'Try Free for 7 Days'. Apple prohibits pricing references in app preview content.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: Your app preview includes references to your app's price. Specifically, the poster frame contains the text 'Try Free for 7 Days.' App previews and screenshots may not reference pricing information, as pricing can vary by region and may change over time.
fix: Removed the 'Try Free for 7 Days' text from the app preview poster frame and video. Replaced it with a value-proposition message like 'Track Your Fitness Goals' that describes app functionality without referencing pricing.

rejectionReason: Your app's version number suggests it is a pre-release or beta version.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted the first version of an app with version number '0.1' or '0.9.2-beta', suggesting the app was not yet a complete, finished product ready for the App Store.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: Your app version number (0.1) suggests that the app is still in development or beta stage. Apps submitted to the App Store should be final versions. Please update your version number to reflect a production-ready release (e.g., 1.0).
fix: Changed the version number from '0.1' to '1.0.0' in the project settings and App Store Connect. Removed any 'beta' or 'pre-release' suffixes from the version string and marketing version.

rejectionReason: Your app appears to have debug or development mode features visible to users.
whatDeveloperDid: Submitted a production build with debug logging visible in a console view, a floating debug menu accessible via a hidden gesture, and network request/response details displayed in an overlay that could be triggered by shaking the device.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: Your app includes development tools and debug information that are visible to users. Specifically, shaking the device reveals a network debugging overlay and there is a hidden debug menu accessible through a multi-tap gesture. Please remove all development/debugging features from the production build.
fix: Wrapped all debug UI elements in #if DEBUG preprocessor directives. Removed the shake-to-debug gesture recognizer from the release build. Ensured the debug menu is only accessible in development builds by checking the build configuration at runtime.

rejectionReason: Your app attempts to connect to localhost or development server URLs.
whatDeveloperDid: Accidentally shipped a production build with the API base URL still pointing to 'http://localhost:3000' or a staging server like 'https://staging-api.example.com'. The app failed to load any content during review because the development server was not accessible.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: Your app was unable to load content during our review. We found that the app is attempting to connect to development/staging server URLs that are not accessible. Please ensure your app connects to production servers.
fix: Fixed the build configuration to use the correct production API URL. Implemented environment-based configuration using .xcconfig files or build schemes so that Debug/Staging/Release builds automatically use the correct server URLs. Added a runtime check that warns during development if production URLs are not configured.

rejectionReason: Your app downloads large amounts of data after installation without informing the user.
whatDeveloperDid: Published a game that was 150MB on download but immediately required an additional 2GB asset download on first launch without any disclosure in the App Store listing or a user prompt before downloading.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.3 - Performance - Accurate Metadata: Your app downloads a significant amount of additional content (approximately 2GB) after installation without informing the user beforehand. Users should be informed about the total storage requirements. Please disclose the additional download requirement in your app description and provide a prompt before downloading.
fix: Added a note in the App Store description about the additional download size. Implemented a pre-download prompt showing the total size and allowing the user to choose when to download. Added a progress indicator showing download status and the option to download over Wi-Fi only.

rejectionReason: Your app has an incomplete user interface. A purchase/subscription page appears blank.
whatDeveloperDid: The app's premium subscription page was blank during review because the StoreKit products hadn't been approved in App Store Connect yet, or the product IDs were misconfigured. The reviewer saw an empty paywall with no pricing or subscription options displayed.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: Your app appears to be incomplete. Specifically, the subscription page does not display any content - it appears as a blank screen with no purchase options. Please ensure all pages in your app are fully functional.
fix: Ensured all IAP products were in 'Ready to Submit' or 'Approved' status in App Store Connect before submitting the app for review. Added error handling and a fallback UI for when StoreKit products fail to load, showing a 'Unable to load products. Please try again.' message with a retry button instead of a blank screen.

rejectionReason: Your app crashed during review due to a system framework issue, but the rejection still stands.
whatDeveloperDid: The app crashed during review due to a known bug in Apple's own UIKit framework (specifically a UICollectionView layout crash on certain iOS versions). The developer filed a Radar/Feedback but the crash still caused a rejection because Apple reviews based on observed behavior, not cause.
whatAppleSaid: Guideline 2.1 - Performance - App Completeness: We discovered one or more bugs in your app. The app crashed when navigating to the gallery view. While we understand this may be related to a system framework issue, apps must not crash regardless of the underlying cause.
fix: Implemented a workaround for the UIKit framework bug by using a different collection view layout approach. Added a try-catch wrapper around the problematic layout code and a fallback layout in case of failure. Filed a Feedback Assistant report with Apple about the underlying framework bug.
